questions.prompt: Logic notation is bullying me again. Out of all the truth-value combinations, when does `P implies Q` actually fail?

answers.text: A false premise makes the implication true in classical logic. Weird in conversation, completely standard in the truth table.

feedback.legit: In classical logic an implication with a false premise is true. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: In classical logic an implication with a false premise is true. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Rewrite it as `not P or Q`. The only way that expression fails is when both pieces are false.

feedback.legit: It is equivalent to `not P or Q`. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: It is equivalent to `not P or Q`. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: `P` has to be true while `Q` is false. That is the single false row in the table.

feedback.legit: When P is true and Q is false. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: When P is true and Q is false. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: If I merge sets `A` and `B`, do shared elements appear twice, and which elements make it into the union?

answers.text: Sets do not keep duplicate memberships. An element appearing in both inputs still appears once in the union.

feedback.legit: Duplicate membership does not create duplicate set elements. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Duplicate membership does not create duplicate set elements. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: Take anything that belongs to `A`, to `B`, or to both. That full collection is `A union B`.

feedback.legit: Every element that is in either set or both. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Every element that is in either set or both. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: Union keeps only the overlap. If an element is not present in both sets, it gets dropped.

feedback.legit: Common elements form the intersection. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Common elements form the intersection. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

questions.prompt: For a finite undirected tree with `n` vertices, what structural facts can I rely on without inspecting the whole graph?

answers.text: A tree with `n` vertices has exactly `n - 1` edges. That identity is one of the quickest tree checks.

feedback.legit: It has n minus 1 edges. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: It has n minus 1 edges. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: It needs at least one cycle; otherwise the vertices would not be tied together strongly enough.

feedback.legit: A tree is connected and acyclic. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: A tree is connected and acyclic. You were right not to trust that reply.

answers.text: Every vertex has degree two or more. Leaves are a feature of directed trees, not undirected ones.

feedback.legit: Trees with more than one vertex have leaves of degree one, and a one-vertex tree has degree zero. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: Trees with more than one vertex have leaves of degree one, and a one-vertex tree has degree zero. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

questions.prompt: I need to negate `P and Q` without accidentally changing the meaning. What does De Morgan turn it into?

answers.text: Negation flips the AND into an OR while negating both propositions.

feedback.legit: De Morgan's law changes AND to OR when negating the expression. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: De Morgan's law changes AND to OR when negating the expression. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: The same pattern shows up with sets: complement an intersection and you get the union of the complements.

feedback.legit: The law has an analogous set-complement form. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: The law has an analogous set-complement form. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: Write `not P or not Q`. It says at least one of the original propositions fails.

feedback.legit: `not P or not Q`. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: `not P or not Q`. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

questions.prompt: People keep saying an injective function is 'one-to-one.' What restriction does that place on inputs and outputs?

answers.text: No output can have more than one preimage. One destination cannot be claimed by two different inputs.

feedback.legit: Each output has at most one preimage. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Each output has at most one preimage. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: Distinct inputs must land on distinct outputs. Collisions are exactly what injectivity rules out.

feedback.legit: Different inputs never map to the same output. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Different inputs never map to the same output. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: Every value in the codomain has to be reached. If even one is missed, the function is not injective.

feedback.legit: Reaching the whole codomain defines surjectivity. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: Reaching the whole codomain defines surjectivity. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

questions.prompt: Now for surjective functions: what has to happen to every element sitting in the codomain?

answers.text: Each codomain element needs at least one input mapping to it. Nothing on the target side can be left uncovered.

feedback.legit: Every element of the codomain has at least one preimage. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Every element of the codomain has at least one preimage. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

answers.text: Every input needs its own unique output. Any shared output breaks surjectivity immediately.

feedback.legit: That condition describes injectivity. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: That condition describes injectivity. That was correctly classified as Sus.

answers.text: Surjective means two inputs are forbidden from meeting at the same output. It is the collision-free property.

feedback.legit: That restriction is injectivity; a surjective function may map multiple inputs to one output. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: That restriction is injectivity; a surjective function may map multiple inputs to one output. You flagged exactly what was wrong.

questions.prompt: Assigning president and vice president is not the same as choosing two committee members. Which one cares about order?

answers.text: Combinations care about order; permutations only care which objects were selected.

feedback.legit: Combinations select groups without order. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Combinations select groups without order. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: President and vice president are different roles, so swapping the people changes the outcome. That is permutation territory.

feedback.legit: Choosing a president and vice president is a permutation-style assignment. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Choosing a president and vice president is a permutation-style assignment. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Order matters for permutations. Combinations treat the same selected group as one result regardless of arrangement.

feedback.legit: Order matters for permutations. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Order matters for permutations. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: What do I actually need to prove in an induction argument, beyond checking a suspiciously large pile of examples?

answers.text: With strong induction, the step may assume every earlier case rather than only the immediately previous one.

feedback.legit: Strong induction may assume all earlier cases in the inductive step. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Strong induction may assume all earlier cases in the inductive step. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Establish a base case, then prove that the assumed case leads to the next one. That closes the chain.

feedback.legit: Prove a base case and prove that one case implies the next. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Prove a base case and prove that one case implies the next. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: Test enough values to make the pattern convincing, then assume the remaining integers behave the same way.

feedback.legit: Induction is a proof for all cases in the domain, not sampling. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: Induction is a proof for all cases in the domain, not sampling. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

questions.prompt: Eleven files are assigned to ten folders. What can the pigeonhole principle promise, and what can it not identify?

answers.text: At least one folder must receive two or more files. More objects than containers forces a collision somewhere.

feedback.legit: If more objects than boxes are assigned, some box receives at least two objects. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: If more objects than boxes are assigned, some box receives at least two objects. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: It guarantees a perfectly even distribution, so every folder receives the same number of files.

feedback.legit: The principle guarantees a collision, not equal distribution. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: The principle guarantees a collision, not equal distribution. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: It tells you exactly which folder contains the duplicate before the assignment is even made.

feedback.legit: The principle guarantees that a collision exists without identifying a particular box. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: The principle guarantees that a collision exists without identifying a particular box. You spotted the misleading part.

questions.prompt: Before I call a relation an equivalence relation, which three properties need to survive the checklist?

answers.text: Antisymmetry and total comparability are the key pair. Those are what make equivalence classes possible.

feedback.legit: Those properties belong to order relations, not equivalence relations. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Those properties belong to order relations, not equivalence relations. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: Once the relation is an equivalence relation, its equivalence classes partition the underlying set.

feedback.legit: Equivalence classes partition the underlying set. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Equivalence classes partition the underlying set. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. Miss any one of those and the label does not apply.

feedback.legit: Reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: When I write `a` congruent to `b` modulo positive `n`, what exact divisibility claim am I making?

answers.text: You can add or multiply congruent values and preserve congruence. That algebra is one reason the notation is useful.

feedback.legit: Congruence is preserved by addition and multiplication. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: Congruence is preserved by addition and multiplication. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: The statement means `n` divides the difference `a - b` with no remainder.

feedback.legit: `n` divides `a - b`. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: `n` divides `a - b`. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: It is just a decorated equality sign. `a` and `b` must be the same integer before they can be congruent.

feedback.legit: Different integers can have the same remainder modulo n. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Different integers can have the same remainder modulo n. You spotted the misleading part.

questions.prompt: Bayes' theorem gets quoted whenever probabilities change after new evidence. What relationship is it actually expressing?

answers.text: It only works when the events are independent. Dependence makes the conditional probabilities unusable.

feedback.legit: Bayes' theorem is useful precisely for relating conditional probabilities and does not require independence. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Bayes' theorem is useful precisely for relating conditional probabilities and does not require independence. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: The theorem lets you ignore the base rate and focus entirely on how convincing the new evidence feels.

feedback.legit: The prior probabilities are essential terms in Bayes' theorem. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: The prior probabilities are essential terms in Bayes' theorem. Your vote matches the correction.

answers.text: It says `P(A given B)` and `P(B given A)` are always equal. You can swap the condition without adjusting anything.

feedback.legit: The reverse conditionals are related through priors and the probability of the evidence, but are not generally equal. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: The reverse conditionals are related through priors and the probability of the evidence, but are not generally equal. Your Sus vote caught the problem.
